pattern: A number made only of 1s can be tracked purely by its remainder mod k, one digit at a time, so pigeonhole guarantees an answer (or a proof there is none) within k steps.
hints: Think about what a repunit (111...1) looks like mod 2 and mod 5 -- its last digit is always 1, so it can never be even or a multiple of 5. That rules out a whole class of k values immediately.
For the rest, you don't need to build the actual (possibly huge) number: track the remainder of the repunit as you extend it one digit at a time, using remainder = (remainder * 10 + 1) mod k.

Start with a length-1 repunit (remainder = 1 mod k) and keep extending; by pigeonhole the remainder must hit 0 within k extensions if it's ever going to, since there are only k possible remainders -- so cap your loop at k iterations and return -1 if none hit 0.

edge_inputs.description: small odd k not multiple of 5
largest k, odd and coprime to 10
k=1 is out of range per constraints so use k=9 needing many digits
k that is a power of a prime coprime to 10

stress.description: sweep many k values coprime to 10 up to a modest bound so both the O(k) remainder walk and the brute-force big-integer oracle finish quickly
stress.note: single k per call; the harness invokes this repeatedly across the pack's stress budget
